result: I keep thinking about the hour just before dawn, when the sky is neither black nor blue but a shade of quiet that hasn’t been named. It’s the only time my apartment building stops humming; the refrigerator forgets its constant song, the elevator ceases its arthritic creak, even the neighbor’s anxious terrier suspends its yap. In that hush I can hear the building remember when this hillside was an apricot orchard, when possums nested where my coffee mugs now stack. The bricks exhale old blossoms, and the floorboards loosen enough that I almost slip through a crack into 1932, land barefoot among fallen fruit and fog. I stay awake to keep the appointment I made as a child: to meet myself on the border between yesterday and tomorrow, to trade secrets across the thin membrane. The child hands me a marble with a galaxy curled inside and asks if I have finally learned to swallow my name without water. I offer a laugh that still smells of yesterday’s garlic, and we negotiate. I get to keep the galaxy if I promise to whistle the orchard’s disappearance every time I boil water. The kettle begins its minor chord, dawn smears lemon across the sill, and the child dissolves like sugar on the tongue of the sun. The terrier resumes its campaign. My pocket, though, is heavier by one small universe, rolling in circles, whispering branch-shadows against porcelain.
